title: The Virgin

jokes.text: Get out of Relationship Free cards... unless the person has an 'Eight More Months of Guilt, Torture and Pain' card
jokes.setup: Wishing for easy breakup cards like Monopoly
jokes.punchline: The other person having a counter-card for prolonged suffering
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational humor with perfect game show parody structure and relatable premise

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, they should make breakup cards like Monopoly cards. 'You have been dumped. Go directly to therapy. Do not pass go. Do not collect two hundred dollars of emotional baggage.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And then the other person has a counter-card: 'Prolonged suffering. Lose three turns and cry in your apartment.'

jokes.text: I think you can take the lift ticket off your jacket now... About six years ago
jokes.setup: George wearing an old ski lift ticket on his jacket
jokes.punchline: Revealing it's six years old but he keeps it to look like a skier
jokes.explanation: Perfect George character moment - pathetic posturing exposed by Jerry's observation

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is that?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's a ski lift ticket from six years ago. I keep it on my jacket so people think I ski.

jokes.text: Women like skiers. So what? You can't meet anybody. You're with Susan.
jokes.setup: George explaining why he keeps the ski ticket to attract women
jokes.punchline: Jerry pointing out he has a girlfriend so it's pointless
jokes.explanation: Solid logical deflation of George's scheme

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Women love a man who skis. It's adventurous, it's dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you have a girlfriend.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're not trying to attract women anymore.

jokes.text: Closet business? What's the closet business? What is it your business?
jokes.setup: Jerry asking about the closet business
jokes.punchline: George turning 'business' into a defensive pun
jokes.explanation: Quick wordplay that flows naturally from the conversation

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So how's the closet business?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not a business, it's a lifestyle.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean it's a lifestyle?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm not in it for the money. I'm in it for the closet.

jokes.text: What do I need more closet space for?
jokes.setup: George explaining why he didn't pursue the closet organizer woman
jokes.punchline: Realizing he doesn't actually need what she's selling
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - shallow motivation exposed by practical reality

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I mean, what do I need a closet organizer for? I don't have anything to organize.

jokes.text: George. Jerry, Marla. Stacy. Stacy. George, Stacy. Jerry. Stacy. George. George. Jerry, Marla. Stacy.
jokes.setup: Attempting to introduce everyone at the coffee shop
jokes.punchline: Names getting completely jumbled and repeated
jokes.explanation: Awkward social moment but lacks the precision of classic Seinfeld bits

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Everyone, I'd like you to meet Marla and Stacy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Marla, Stacy, this is Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: Nice to meet you Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Stacy
dialogue.line: Hi Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And George, this is Marla and Stacy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: We just met.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Right, right. Marla, Stacy, George. George, Marla, Stacy.

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: Is he okay?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I think he's having a stroke.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Stacy, Marla, George, Marla, Stacy, George, Jerry!

jokes.text: I was watching it on CNN and they covered it so well, I thought, why knock my brains out?
jokes.setup: George lying about going to Berlin for the wall coming down
jokes.punchline: Deciding TV coverage was good enough so he didn't need to go
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly lazy George logic - treating historic moment like a TV special to skip

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I was going to go to Berlin for the wall coming down, but then I thought, why? I saw it on TV. I got all the satisfaction of going without the aggravation of actually having to go.

jokes.text: I don't know. He never came back.
jokes.setup: Discussing her boyfriend's trip to Berlin
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that he disappeared
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of shocking information treated as mundane

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: He went to Berlin.

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: He's been there for three weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: I haven't heard from him.

jokes.text: Some people write symphonies. This is my gift.
jokes.setup: George talking about how writing comes easily to him
jokes.punchline: Comparing sitcom writing to composing symphonies
jokes.explanation: Peak George delusion - elevating his minimal talent to artistic genius level

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Writing? It's easy. I sit down, the words just flow out. It's like I'm Mozart or something.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: These sitcom writers, they think they're composing symphonies. They're not. They're just... typing jokes.

jokes.text: Is there any Tampax in your house? Yeah. You got a girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Jerry using various relationship markers to diagnose George's status
jokes.punchline: Tampax being the definitive proof of girlfriend status
jokes.explanation: Clever relationship diagnostic with unexpected intimate product as the clincher

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I think I can tell if a guy has a girlfriend by looking at his apartment. There's certain things... signs.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What signs?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, there's pictures on the nightstand, books on the coffee table, plants... fruit in a bowl.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I have fruit in a bowl.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, but is it real fruit or decorative fruit?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What's the difference?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Real fruit rots. Decorative fruit just sits there. Anyway, the real test - the definitive proof - Tampax in the bathroom.

jokes.text: Once you can get girlfriends, you just want more girlfriends.
jokes.setup: Discussing the irony of having job success but being tied down
jokes.punchline: The circular logic of relationship desire
jokes.explanation: Solid relationship observation with George's typical circular reasoning

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is the thing about relationships. The more successful you become, the more trapped you feel.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what are you saying?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm saying the woman doesn't want you when you have nothing. But the second you have something, she doesn't want to let you go.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So it's a catch-22.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly! You can't win! You're damned if you do, damned if you don't.

jokes.text: You're gonna make a very good father someday.
jokes.setup: George's completely selfish relationship philosophy
jokes.punchline: Jerry sarcastically praising his parenting potential
jokes.explanation: Clean sarcastic response to George's selfishness

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, George, with an attitude like that, you're going to make a wonderful father someday.

jokes.text: He works at NBC. I work at NBC... They fall madly in love and she dumps me for David Letterman.
jokes.setup: George's plan to get Letterman to date Susan
jokes.punchline: The elaborate scheme to orchestrate his own dumping
jokes.explanation: Hilariously convoluted George scheme with celebrity involvement

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I got it. I got it. I'll get her to break up with me.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'll start dating someone else. She'll find out, get angry, and dump me.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Then I call Letterman, tell him I'm available, and boom — I'm on the show.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's your plan? That's insane.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's foolproof! Susan will be so mad she won't even want to see me again.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Perfect setup for Letterman.

jokes.text: I don't think you are.
jokes.setup: George claiming he's 'just thinking' about the Letterman plan
jokes.punchline: Jerry's flat dismissal of George's mental capacity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry deflation with minimal words maximum impact

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm just thinking about the Letterman plan.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, there's your problem right there.

jokes.text: We'll put everything on hooks... You're quite mad, you know.
jokes.setup: Marla's radical closet organization plan using only hooks
jokes.punchline: Jerry's understated British assessment of her sanity
jokes.explanation: Absurd organizational scheme met with perfect dry British response

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: Everything on hooks. That's the system. Clothes, shoes, bags, everything.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're saying... all hooks, all the time?

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: Yes! It's revolutionary. No drawers, no shelves, just hooks.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Right. Well, that's... that's certainly a choice. A choice that someone might make.

jokes.text: It was destroying my brain cells... but now you're in here all the time
jokes.setup: Jerry gave away his TV to avoid addiction
jokes.punchline: Now Kramer is constantly at Jerry's watching it
jokes.explanation: Classic unintended consequences humor with Kramer invasion

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hey Jerry, what's with no TV? You sick or something?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I gave it away. I was watching too much.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You gave it away? What am I supposed to do now?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, Kramer. Read a book?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A book? What am I, your grandmother? I'm coming over to watch your TV.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't have a TV anymore, remember?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, get one. I'll be here at eight.

jokes.text: There's weather. I don't need weather. Weather doesn't do it for me.
jokes.setup: Jerry suggesting Kramer go outside
jokes.punchline: Kramer dismissing weather as insufficient entertainment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer absurdism - treating natural phenomena like entertainment options

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why don't you go outside?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's raining.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So? I'm not going out in the rain just to stand around getting wet. I need a reason to go outside. I need a destination.

jokes.text: Oh, you must have been devastated being left for a wall.
jokes.setup: Marla's boyfriend never returned from Berlin
jokes.punchline: Framing it as being dumped for the Berlin Wall
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reframing of historical moment as romantic rival - peak Seinfeld wordplay

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So her boyfriend never came back from Berlin? That's rough.

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: I know! He just disappeared.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, at least you can say you were dumped for the Berlin Wall. That's a good story.

jokes.text: Well, because I'm a... virgin.
jokes.setup: Building tension about why she wouldn't move in with boyfriend
jokes.punchline: The awkward revelation of virginity
jokes.explanation: Well-built awkward reveal that sets up episode's central premise

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: I can't move in with him.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: Because I'm a virgin.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're a virgin?

dialogue.speaker: Marla
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How is that possible?

jokes.text: Now it cost me $35 to see Havana.
jokes.setup: Elaine returning a video tape two weeks late
jokes.punchline: The exorbitant late fee exceeding the movie's value
jokes.explanation: Relatable video store late fee complaint with good specificity

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I want to return this video tape. I know it's a couple weeks late.

dialogue.speaker: Video Store Clerk
dialogue.line: That'll be $47.50 in late fees.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Forty-seven dollars? The movie cost eight bucks!

dialogue.speaker: Video Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Late fees accumulate daily.

jokes.text: And my diaphragm goes flying out... So I just froze... it's just lying there
jokes.setup: Elaine at a party throwing her purse down
jokes.punchline: Diaphragm flying out in front of a guy she's talking to
jokes.explanation: Classic awkward Elaine oversharing with physical comedy element

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh no!

dialogue.speaker: Guy
dialogue.line: What is that?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's... that's my diaphragm.

jokes.text: You never know when you're gonna need it.
jokes.setup: Defending carrying a diaphragm everywhere
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact preparedness about contraception
jokes.explanation: Elaine's practical but inappropriate oversharing continues

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You never know when you're going to meet someone. I could be at the grocery store, I could be at a movie theater. I like to be prepared.

jokes.text: Well, it's not like spotting a toupee.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining he didn't know Marla was a virgin
jokes.punchline: Comparing virginity detection to toupee spotting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry logic with absurd comparison delivered deadpan

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How was I supposed to know? It's not like there's a big sign. You know, it's not like a toupee where you can spot it right away. 'Oh, that's a toupee!' No, virginity, you can't tell. There should be something. There should be a little indicator light that goes off.

jokes.text: I don't wanna be remembered. I wanna be forgotten.
jokes.setup: Discussing being someone's first sexual experience
jokes.punchline: Jerry's preference for sexual anonymity
jokes.explanation: Perfectly selfish Jerry philosophy about romantic responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't want to be someone's first. I don't want that pressure. I want to be like the third or fourth. That way, they already know what they're doing, and I don't have to explain anything.

jokes.text: You know, sometimes those guys don't make it back.
jokes.setup: George needing pioneer spirit like Lewis and Clark
jokes.punchline: Some explorers die on their missions
jokes.explanation: Dark extension of exploration metaphor with perfect historical accuracy

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you need that pioneer spirit, like Lewis and Clark.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Lewis and Clark? What do you want me to do?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Some explorers die on their missions.

jokes.text: It's a big, flat noodle. Well, I don't want a big, flat noodle.
jokes.setup: Explaining what chow fun is over the phone
jokes.punchline: Jerry's immediate rejection based on noodle dimensions
jokes.explanation: Absurd food specificity that escalates into pickiness

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I'm not eating anything called chow fun.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because I don't like the sound of it. Chow fun? It sounds like something you'd order at a pervert's restaurant.

jokes.text: Who says I want a noodle?
jokes.setup: Discussion of what kind of noodle Jerry wants
jokes.punchline: Jerry rejecting the entire noodle concept
jokes.explanation: Jerry's contrarian escalation of food ordering dispute

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't want a noodle. I want a real meal. You're offering me a noodle like it's some kind of culinary achievement. It's a noodle! It's string! You boil string!

jokes.text: They all turn into farmers suddenly.
jokes.setup: Men always claiming they need to get up early after sex
jokes.punchline: Comparing them to farmers with early schedules
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor for male post-sex behavior with perfect specificity

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: They're like farmers! 'I gotta get up at 5 AM, I got cows to milk.' No you don't! You work at a desk!

jokes.text: So the judge decrees that he becomes my butler.
jokes.setup: Uninsured motorist can't pay for accident damage
jokes.punchline: Legal system ordering him to become personal servant
jokes.explanation: Hilariously absurd legal solution that becomes the episode's subplot

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So the judge says I have to be his personal servant? For how long?

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Until the debt is paid in full.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is involuntary servitude! This is illegal!

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: You should have had insurance.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm gonna be someone's slave because of a fender bender?

jokes.text: Sentenced to be a butler.
jokes.setup: George's elaborate butler scheme explanation
jokes.punchline: Jerry's flat dismissal of the legal premise
jokes.explanation: Jerry's perfect straight-man response highlighting the absurdity

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, it's foolproof. I hire a butler, he lives in my apartment, handles all my affairs, and I pay him minimum wage. It's like having a personal assistant but completely legal.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, that's not legal.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because you just described indentured servitude.

jokes.text: All you saved was the pea pods?
jokes.setup: Ping could only save one bag from the accident
jokes.punchline: It was specifically George's enemy dish
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to earlier food conflict with ironic outcome

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It was specifically George's enemy dish

jokes.text: You jaywalk. I can slap a suit on you.
jokes.setup: Accident discussion turning into blame game
jokes.punchline: Injured delivery boy threatening legal action
jokes.explanation: Minor character assertiveness leading to episode's legal subplot

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You hit him with your car!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Me? You told me to back up!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I said go forward!

dialogue.speaker: Delivery Boy
dialogue.line: I'm calling my lawyer!

jokes.text: One bad impression, you're out of the business!
jokes.setup: George panicking about the NBC meeting
jokes.punchline: Dramatic overstatement of entertainment industry consequences
jokes.explanation: Peak George paranoia and industry melodrama

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Do you realize what this means? If this meeting goes badly, I could be blacklisted! Blacklisted! I'll never work in this town again. Never! I'll have to move to... to Portland or something. I'll be making lattes at some coffee shop, living in a studio apartment with three roommates, eating ramen noodles for the rest of my life!

jokes.text: She was laughing and then they shot her! They shoot you for laughing!
jokes.setup: George fabricating a sister's death excuse
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd details about urban violence
jokes.explanation: George's panic-induced absurd storytelling at its peak

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My sister was killed by an immigrant.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, he was just driving around looking for somebody to kill.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: He didn't know her. It was completely random.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: He just saw her on the street and he killed her.

jokes.text: Yes, I was very wise to hitch my wagon to his star.
jokes.setup: Jerry going into meeting while George stays behind
jokes.punchline: George's delusional assessment of their partnership
jokes.explanation: Classic George self-importance while being completely useless

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry, I'm telling you, I have a good feeling about this. This is the one. I can feel it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: We're a team. You and me. We're like a well-oiled machine. I do the thinking, you do the talking.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: They're gonna love us. We walk in there, we're gonna own that room.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is it. This is what we've been waiting for. Our shot.

jokes.text: And you bump into somebody you know.
jokes.setup: Pitching the Chinese restaurant waiting concept
jokes.punchline: Adding awkward social encounter to the waiting premise
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor as Jerry pitches what becomes a real Seinfeld episode

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So we're waiting for a table at a Chinese restaurant. And while we're waiting, we run into someone we know, but we're not really friends with them. So there's that awkward thing where we have to make conversation, but we don't really want to.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, and they keep asking us questions about our lives, and we have to pretend we care.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly. We're trapped. We can't leave because we're waiting for the table, and we can't ignore them because they saw us.

jokes.text: Those collars are funny. You see somebody in one of those, you start laughing immediately.
jokes.setup: George expanding on the butler show idea
jokes.punchline: Commentary on physical comedy props
jokes.explanation: Meta-comedy observation about visual humor devices

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The butler could trip over things. You know, physical comedy. A vase breaks, he slips on the water...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But here's the thing - every time he falls, he's gotta catch himself on something. A chair, a table, the wall. Because if he just goes down, that's slapstick. But if he's struggling to stay up, that's character.

jokes.text: Next time I should probably break the Prozacs in half.
jokes.setup: Letterman rejecting George's relationship scheme
jokes.punchline: Letterman suggesting George needs less medication
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about mental health medication delivered as celebrity dismissal

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'less medication'? I'm not on any medication.

dialogue.speaker: David Letterman
dialogue.line: Well, maybe you should be. Or maybe you should be on less of it.

jokes.text: Who is Joseph Cotten? Giddyup.
jokes.setup: Kramer watching Jeopardy on Jerry's TV
jokes.punchline: His confident incorrect answers and celebration
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer overconfidence in game show participation

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What is the Magna Carta?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yes! I'm on fire!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Who is Napoleon Bonaparte?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, I got it!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What is a Bundt cake?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Ha! I'm the best!

jokes.text: You are a stupid, stupid man.
jokes.setup: Susan explaining she got fired because George kissed her
jokes.punchline: Her direct assessment of George's intelligence
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character beat showing Susan's justified anger

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: He's not a moron. He's only a case study.

jokes.text: Finally, my stupidity pays off!
jokes.setup: George learning Susan got fired
jokes.punchline: Celebrating his mistake as strategic success
jokes.explanation: Peak George self-awareness and celebration of his own incompetence

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She got fired? Susan got fired?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I didn't think it was possible. I mean, she's a network executive. How do you get fired from that?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... I got her fired. Me. I did that.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm a bad employee and a bad boyfriend, but I'm an excellent bad influence.

jokes.text: Every time I think I'm out, they pull me back in.
jokes.setup: Learning he can't break up with Susan after getting her fired
jokes.punchline: Godfather reference about being trapped in the relationship
jokes.explanation: Classic movie reference perfectly applied to George's romantic entrapment

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's like The Godfather. Just when I thought I was out, they pull me back in!

jokes.text: She comes from a broken home. A tree fell on the roof and cracked the structure.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining Elaine's relationship advice isn't trustworthy
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of 'broken home'
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection joke with brilliant literal interpretation of common phrase

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, Elaine's giving relationship advice? That's like taking financial tips from a guy living in a cardboard box.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She comes from a broken home.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I mean, literally broken. The door doesn't close, the sink leaks... I think there's a hole in the wall.

jokes.text: Since she met him, she's been vomited on, her cabin burned down, learned her father's a homosexual and got fired. Yeah, they had a real good thing going.
jokes.setup: Discussing George and Susan's 'good relationship'
jokes.punchline: Cataloging all of Susan's disasters since dating George
jokes.explanation: Brilliant series callback showing George as harbinger of disaster

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Susan's had a great relationship with George.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? She's been a disaster since they started dating.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Name one thing.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: One thing? Her job, her apartment, her car, her family — everything has fallen apart.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not George's fault.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, she met him and her life imploded. That's cause and effect.

jokes.text: How can you write that crap?
jokes.setup: Jerry using his sitcom writer status to meet women
jokes.punchline: Woman dismissing sitcoms as garbage
jokes.explanation: Self-deprecating meta-humor about the show itself

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm a sitcom writer.

dialogue.speaker: Woman at bar
dialogue.line: Sitcoms are garbage.

jokes.text: There's a first date you'll never forget... 'Not well, Chuck. Not well.'
jokes.setup: Ancient cultures sacrificing virgins to volcanoes
jokes.punchline: Imagining it as a game show in heaven
jokes.explanation: Dark historical humor combined with game show format parody - classic Jerry closer

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're telling me ancient cultures were sacrificing virgins to volcanoes? That's insane. I mean, imagine if that was a game show in heaven.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: "Welcome back to Volcano Roulette! Our next contestant, a 23-year-old virgin from Peru. Tell us, what do you do for a living?"

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: "Oh, you're a shepherd? Well, let's see what you've won today... A TRIP DOWN THE VOLCANO!"

tmdb_overview: Jerry courts a woman with a tame past; George makes a bad move at a meeting; a man disagrees with Elaine.
insight: Escalation-driven episode lands 46 jokes in 22 minutes but struggles with impact delivery.
